title: How to Measure PD for Glasses: Pupillary Distance Guide
description: Learn how to measure your pupillary distance (PD) for ordering glasses. We explain what PD is, why it matters, DIY methods, and when professional measurement is essential.
tldr: Pupillary distance (PD) is the distance between the centres of your pupils, measured in millimetres. Average adult PD is 54–74 mm. You can measure at home using a mirror and ruler, or with a phone app, but professional measurement is more accurate — especially for progressive lenses or strong prescriptions.

definition.text: The distance in millimetres between the centres of the left and right pupils when looking straight ahead. PD is used to align the optical centres of prescription lenses with your eyes, ensuring clear, comfortable vision. It can be expressed as a single number (binocular PD) or two numbers (monocular PD — each eye measured from the nose bridge).

sections.content:
Every prescription lens has an optical centre — the point where light passes through with zero distortion. When the optical centre of your lens aligns perfectly with your pupil, you get the clearest, most comfortable vision.

If the PD is off by even 2–3 mm, you might not notice much with a mild prescription. But with stronger prescriptions (+/-3.00 and above), even small PD errors cause prismatic effect — your eyes have to work harder to converge, leading to headaches, eye strain, and blurred peripheral vision.

For progressive lenses, PD accuracy is even more critical. The progressive corridor (the narrow channel of clear vision) must align precisely with your pupils for all three zones (distance, intermediate, near) to work properly.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

sections.h2: How to Measure PD at Home (Mirror Method)
sections.content:
You'll need a millimetre ruler and a mirror. A friend with steady hands works even better than a mirror.

Mirror method:

  1. Stand about 20 cm (8 inches) from a mirror
  2. Hold a millimetre ruler against your brow, just above your eyes
  3. Close your right eye and align the ruler's zero mark with the centre of your left pupil
  4. Without moving the ruler, close your left eye and open your right eye
  5. Read the measurement at the centre of your right pupil
  6. Repeat 3 times and average the results

Friend method (more accurate):

  1. Face your friend at arm's length
  2. Hold the ruler against your brow
  3. Have your friend look at a distant object over your shoulder (this keeps their eyes focused at distance, preventing convergence)
  4. Your friend measures from the centre of one pupil to the centre of the other
  5. Repeat 3 times

Single (binocular) PD: One number representing the total distance between both pupils. Example: 63 mm. Sufficient for most single vision glasses.

Dual (monocular) PD: Two numbers representing each eye's distance from the centre of the nose bridge. Example: 31.5 / 31.5 mm (symmetrical) or 30 / 32 mm (asymmetrical). Most people are slightly asymmetrical.

Dual PD is more precise and important for progressive lenses, strong prescriptions, and high-quality optics. When an optician measures your PD at an eye exam, they typically record the dual measurement. If you're ordering online, most sites accept either format.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

Several apps claim to measure PD using your phone camera. They work by using a reference object (like a credit card held to your forehead) for scale, then detecting your pupils in a photo.

The results are generally within 1–2 mm of professional measurement — acceptable for mild single vision prescriptions but not ideal for progressives. The accuracy depends heavily on following instructions precisely, holding the phone at the correct distance, and having good lighting.

Popular options include EyeMeasure (iOS), GlassesOn (iOS/Android), and PD Meter (Android). They're a reasonable alternative when professional measurement isn't available, but treat them as approximations.

Measure at home or with an app for simple single vision orders under +/-3.00. Get professional measurement for:

  • Progressive lenses: PD accuracy of ±0.5 mm is ideal for progressives. Home measurement typically achieves ±1–2 mm.
  • Strong prescriptions (+/-4.00+): The prismatic effect of misaligned optical centres increases with prescription power.
  • First-time glasses: You have no reference point for what feels right. Start with professional measurements.
  • Children: Kids' faces are smaller, PDs are narrower, and accurate measurement is harder without proper equipment.

An optician uses a pupillometer — a device that measures PD precisely while you focus on a target at the correct distance. This eliminates the convergence error that makes home measurement tricky. At Charm Optical, PD measurement is part of the standard fitting process when you order glasses.

sections.h2: Common PD Questions When Ordering Online
sections.content:
Online glasses retailers require you to enter your PD during checkout. A few things to know:

  • Your optometrist may or may not include PD on your prescription — it's not legally required in most Canadian provinces. If it's missing, ask for it.
  • PD doesn't change much after age 18. If you have it from a previous exam, it's likely still accurate.
  • If a site asks for "near PD" and "distance PD," near PD is typically 3–4 mm less than distance PD (your eyes converge when looking at close objects)
  • If you only have a single PD, you can estimate monocular by dividing by two (though this assumes perfect symmetry, which is uncommon)

For accurate online orders, getting your PD professionally measured once is the best investment. It takes 30 seconds, costs nothing during an eye exam, and you can use it for years. Check your insurance for exam coverage.

Your glasses are only as good as your prescription. An outdated or inaccurate prescription means blurry vision no matter how expensive your lenses are. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults and annually for children, seniors, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease.

A comprehensive eye exam does far more than measure your prescription. Your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels — catching conditions like glaucoma, macular degeneration, and diabetic retinopathy early, often before you notice any symptoms. They also measure your intraocular pressure, test your peripheral vision, and assess how your eyes work together as a team.

In Alberta, a comprehensive eye exam typically costs $100-$180 if not covered by insurance. Most employer plans cover the exam fee entirely. Children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health. faqs.q: Is PD included on my prescription?
faqs.a: Not always. In most Canadian provinces, PD isn't a required element of an eyeglass prescription. Many optometrists include it voluntarily; some don't because they consider it a fitting measurement rather than a prescription component. Ask for it specifically during your exam.
PD stabilizes by about age 18–20 and stays essentially the same throughout adulthood. Children's PD changes as their face grows, so remeasurement at each exam is important.

faqs.q: What happens if my PD is wrong on my glasses?
faqs.a: With mild prescriptions, you might not notice. With stronger prescriptions, incorrect PD causes the optical centres to misalign with your pupils, creating a prismatic effect. Symptoms include headaches, eye strain, blurred or double vision at the periphery, and difficulty focusing.

faqs.q: Can I measure PD from my old glasses?
faqs.a: You can measure the distance between the centre of each lens in your current glasses as an approximation. Use a ruler to measure from the centre of the left lens to the centre of the right lens. This gives you the fitted PD, which should be close to your actual PD if the glasses were made correctly.

faqs.q: How often should I get my eyes checked?
faqs.a: Adults should get a comprehensive eye exam every 1-2 years. Children, seniors, diabetics, and those with family history of eye disease should go annually. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists — book online at see.charmoptical.ca.

faqs.q: Can I use my prescription to buy glasses anywhere?
faqs.a: Yes, in Canada your prescription belongs to you and you can use it at any optical store. However, progressive lenses require additional measurements (PD, segment height) that must be taken in person. Charm Optical takes these measurements for free during your frame selection.
